title: Dovetail pine keepsake box
subtitle: A hand-cut dovetail box in seasoned pine with a loose lid
excerpt: A small box with hand-cut through-dovetail corners is the classic exercise in precise woodworking. The dovetail joint is strong, self-aligning during assembly, and unmistakably hand-made. A first box in pine teaches every dovetail skill: marking, sawing to a line, chopping waste, and fitting. Once the method is understood, it transfers to any species and any box size.
sourceNotes: Through-dovetail joint construction documented in Moxon's Mechanick Exercises (1703), Holtzapffel's Turning and Mechanical Manipulation (1843), and in British furniture and joinery craft traditions.

content.text: A hand-cut
dovetail
joint is not as hard as its reputation. The main skill is sawing straight to a line and
paring
to the
baseline

. Pine is the right wood to start on. It is soft enough that the chisel cuts easily. The dovetail shoulders forgive small slips. The grain is easy to read, which helps you saw to the line. A first box in pine teaches every step you would use later for oak or walnut.
What you need: wood

Seasoned pine boards, 12 mm thick, for the sides; a slightly thicker board (15 to 18 mm) for the base and lid if a recessed lid is planned. For a box 18 cm long by 12 cm wide by 8 cm tall: two side pieces at 18 cm x 8 cm, two end pieces at 12 cm x 8 cm, one base at 18 cm x 12 cm (glued into a rebate or floating in a groove).
Seasoned
wood is non-negotiable: movement in unseasoned pine will open the joints.
What you need: tools

A tenon saw with a fine tooth for cutting the dovetail slopes. A coping saw for removing the waste between tails and pins. A bench chisel (12 mm) and a carpenter's mallet for paring. A marking gauge set to the board thickness for scribing the baseline on both faces. A marking knife for scribing the tail and pin angles.
The work
Step 1: Mark and cut the tails

Set the marking gauge to the board thickness (12 mm) and scribe the baseline around the end of each tail board. Mark the tail angles on the end grain: a 1:6 slope for pine (wider softwood tails need a shallower angle than hardwood). Mark two tails on a 12 mm thick side board leaves room for two full tails and two half-pins at the corners. Saw down the tail cheeks with the tenon saw, keeping the blade on the waste side of the knife line. Remove the waste between tails with the coping saw, then pare to the baseline with the chisel.
Step 2: Transfer and cut the pins

Place the cut tail board on the end of the pin board and scribe the tail profile directly onto the pin board end grain with the marking knife. This transfers the exact tail positions. Saw the pin cheeks with the tenon saw on the waste side. Remove waste with the coping saw and pare to the baseline.
Step 3: Test fit and adjust

Offer the joint together by hand; it should slide together with firm hand pressure, not require a mallet, and not rock. Any tight spot will show as a shiny burnish on the wood where it is high; pare that spot with the chisel. Any gap shows which face needs material removed from the other side. Work from both boards alternately until the joint goes together cleanly and the shoulder lines are tight all the way across.
Step 4: Assemble all four corners

Cut and fit all four corner joints before gluing anything. A dry fit of all four corners confirms the box shape is square and the joints all meet at the baseline. Apply PVA or hide glue to all joint faces, assemble, and clamp square. Check squareness with a try square across the diagonals: equal diagonals confirm a square box. Leave to cure for at least four hours.
Step 5: Fit the base and lid

The simplest base is a flat panel glued or nailed into a rebate cut into the lower inside edge of the box sides. The lid can be a flat panel sitting in a stopped rebate at the top, cut with the tenon saw and chisel. A loose lid that rests in the opening without a hinge is the simplest form for a first box.
Sanding and finishing

Once the glue has cured, sand the outside surfaces through 120, 180, and 240 grit. Clean up the dovetail corners with a sharp chisel rather than sanding if there is any protrusion. Apply raw linseed oil. Cure for two weeks. The box can be used for storage immediately after gluing; the oil finish is for protection and appearance.
Variations
Use lime or poplar in place of pine for an even softer, more forgiving first box; both species are sold as carving blanks and are typically well-seasoned.
Add a small pyrography motif to the lid surface after sanding for a personalised keepsake box.
Make a larger box (30 cm x 20 cm) as a jewellery or letter box once the first small box has confirmed the technique.

attrs.intro: Problems with dovetail fit and box squareness.

items.fix: Identify which face has the gap. Pare the base of the joint on the opposite face to allow the shoulder to close. In future, check cheek cuts with a try square before paring.
items.cause: The tail or pin cheek was not cut square to the board face; it leans slightly inward or outward.
items.symptom: The joint goes together but the shoulder line has a gap on one face

items.fix: While the glue is still wet, loosen one clamp and adjust it. Re-check diagonals. A sash clamp across the diagonals can pull a box square after assembly.
items.cause: Clamp pressure pushed one side out; the clamp was not placed symmetrically across the joint.
items.symptom: The box is out of square after clamping

items.fix: Use a finer-tooth tenon saw (12 to 14 tpi). Reduce pressure and let the saw do the work. Score the shoulder line deeply with a marking knife before sawing to sever the surface fibres before the saw reaches them.
items.cause: The saw is being pushed too hard, or the saw teeth are too coarse for the thin board.
items.symptom: Pine tearout on the tail cheeks when sawing

attrs.heading: Common problems
